text: This paper investigates the occurrence of manifest interparty conflict on policy issues and the functions of coalition agreements as a means of conflict resolution. Based on our empirical research into the structure of coalition agreements in relation to policy compromises and the conflicts occurring during the terms of office of coalition governments in Germany, Belgium, Italy and the Netherlands, our analysis provides insight into the scope of binding influence of formally and contractually documented policy issues for the participating parties. Our analysis shows that all coalition agreements in our sample of four countries contain policy obligations for the governing parties, approximately one-third of them in highly specified form. Both agreed and contentious issues are covered, and our core finding is that parties can always refer back to the contractual terms of the agreement in case of intra-governmental disagreement whenever this is possible due to the presence of corresponding clauses in the coalition agreement. Thus the policy content of a coalition agreement has a regulative function that is conducive to the stable operation of parties in very different coalition constellations.
explanation: The text is a well-structured, formal abstract with domain-specific content; while it could be human-written, its generic analytical phrasing and lack of distinctive authorial voice leave AI-generated possibility plausible.
